contest.title: 2025-2026 ICPC Asia Taichung Regional Contest (Unrated, Online Mirror, ICPC Rules, Teams Preferred)

sources.notes: Construct a simple connected graph with exactly k odd-degree vertices and b bridges.
Writeup builds graphs from 2-edge-connected components, cycles, stars, and chains with case analysis.
Alternative notes classify cases by comparing b+1 and k.

annotation.summary: Construct a simple connected graph on n vertices with exactly k odd-degree vertices and exactly b bridges, or prove impossible.
annotation.constraints: Total n up to 1e5 and output edges up to 5n, so the construction must be explicit and linear.
annotation.core_idea: By the handshaking lemma k must be even. Contracting 2-edge-connected components turns the bridge structure into a tree with b+1 components. Use cycles as bridge-free components, stars/chains to create required bridge leaves, and add chords inside a large cycle to create extra odd-degree vertices when k is larger than the number forced by bridges. Small cases such as n=2, b=n-2, or a 3-cycle with required chords need separate handling to keep the graph simple.
tricks: case split on b+1 versus k

solution_variants.name: bridge-block-constructive-cases
solution_variants.summary: Construct cycles as 2-edge-connected blocks and connect them by bridges according to the desired bridge count and odd-degree count.

tags.evidence: The problem asks for an explicit graph and the solution is a case-based construction.
The construction is organized around exactly b bridge edges and 2-edge-connected blocks.
tags.description: Use graph bridges, bridge trees, or 2-edge-connected components as a central concept.
tags.created_reason: Bridge/edge-biconnected structure is a common graph subtopic not yet represented.

tags.evidence: The handshaking lemma gives the parity feasibility condition on k.
The construction splits into b=0, b+1>k, b+1=k, and b+1<k cases plus corner cases.
tags.description: A solution where correctness depends on a small number of structural cases and boundary cases.
tags.created_reason: Many constructive problems are best retrieved by their required case analysis.
